title: Human Eye and Colourful World
introduction: This lesson explores the structure of the human eye, its function, and how it perceives colors. Understanding the eye's role in our perception of the colorful world is crucial.
visual_description: The diagram shows the basic structure of the human eye, including the cornea, iris, pupil, lens, vitreous humor, retina, optic nerve, and choroid.

sections.heading: Structure of the Human Eye
sections.content: The human eye is a complex organ that consists of various parts working together to focus light on the retina for vision.

sections.heading: How We See Colors
sections.content: Colors are perceived due to the presence of photoreceptor cells called cones in the retina, which respond differently to red, green, and blue light.

activity: Conduct a simple experiment by observing color mixing using a prism or diffraction grating. This will help students understand how white light can be separated into different colors.
summary: Understanding the human eye's structure and function, as well as the perception of colors, provides valuable insights into our visual system and the world around us.
key_facts: The human eye contains about 6-7 million cones for color vision.
Rods in the eye are responsible for night vision but do not contribute to color perception.
White light is a combination of red, green, and blue light.
